s: Mandela's prison, now run by ex-prisoners
w: For 18 of his 27 imprisoned years, Nelson Mandela broke rocks in the courtyard here. The island served as a leper colony from 1845 to 1931 before becoming a maximum-security political prison in 1961. UNESCO-listed in 1999; tours are led by former inmates.
_facts: Leper colony 1845-1931
Political prison from 1961
Mandela held here 18 of 27 imprisoned years
UNESCO World Heritage Site 1999
Former prisoners serve as guides

s: The headland that unlocked the sea route to Asia
w: Bartolomeu Dias rounded this rocky headland in February 1488 — the first European to do so — calling it 'Cabo das Tormentas' (Cape of Storms). King John II of Portugal renamed it: 'Good Hope' for the sea route to India it promised. The legend stuck.
_facts: Dias rounded it February 1488, first European to do so
Originally named Cabo das Tormentas (Cape of Storms)
Renamed Cape of Good Hope by King John II of Portugal
Unlocked the sea route from Europe to Asia

s: Africa's only Martello tower — never armed
w: Built by the British in 1796 on an 8-metre-high drum with 1.8-metre-thick walls, this is the only Martello tower on the African continent. It was never armed. By 1843 it was whitewashed instead as a navigation beacon; restored in 1972 as a small museum.
_facts: Built by British 1796
Only Martello tower in Africa
8m high, 13m base diameter, 1.8m walls
Never armed; whitewashed 1843 as navigation beacon
Restored 1972 by Simon's Town Historical Society, provincial heritage site

w: 'Our Efforts' in Xhosa — also known as Mandela Park — Imizamo Yethu packs some 33,600 residents into 18 hectares on the Hout Bay hillside. In March 2017 a fire swept through, killing three people and destroying around 3,500 homes in a single night.
_facts: Xhosa name means 'Our Efforts'
Also known as Mandela Park
18 hectares in Hout Bay valley
Approximately 33,600 residents
2017 fire killed 3, destroyed approximately 3,500 homes on March 11-12

s: Cape Town's oldest farm, run by a woman in 1688
w: The oldest farm in Cape Town dates to 1682. German immigrant Catharina Ras secured the land deed in 1688, among the earliest female landowners at the Cape. Frederick Russouw planted the first vines in 1695; by 1735 there were 30,000. The manor is a national monument.
_facts: Established 1682, oldest farm in Cape Town
Catharina Ras secured land deed 1688, one of earliest female landowners at Cape
Frederick Russouw planted first vines 1695
30,000 vines by 1735
Manor house declared national monument 1983

s: South Africa's oldest place of Christian worship
w: A church has stood on this corner since 1678. The current neoclassical building replaced it in 1841, though the original tower survives. Its carved pulpit, by sculptor Anton Anreith and carpenter Jacob Graaff, was installed in 1789. South Africa's largest church organ followed in 1954.
_facts: First church built 1678
South Africa's oldest place of Christian worship
Current neoclassical building 1841 by Herman Schuette
Original tower preserved
Pulpit by Anton Anreith and Jacob Graaff, installed 1789
Largest church organ in South Africa installed 1954

n: Royal Observatory, Cape of Good Hope
s: First scientific institution in Africa, est. 1820
w: Founded in 1820 by Britain's Board of Longitude, this was the first scientific institution on the African continent. Its astronomers remotely triggered the Noon Gun on Signal Hill to synchronise ships' chronometers in Table Bay. The original main building still stands.
_facts: Established 1820 by British Board of Longitude
First scientific institution in Africa
Remotely fired the Signal Hill Noon Gun for maritime time synchronisation
Astronomers Thomas Maclear (Royal Medal 1869) and David Gill (Royal Medal 1903) worked here

s: Nine kilometres cut into a vertical cliff face
w: Carved 1915 to 1922 into near-vertical cliff more than 200 metres above the Atlantic, linking Hout Bay to Noordhoek. Rockfalls in the 1990s closed the road for years; it was redesigned and reopened as a toll road in 2005. Each bend opens a different angle on the bay.
_facts: Carved into near-vertical cliff 200m+ above Atlantic
Links Hout Bay to Noordhoek
Rockfalls in 1990s caused closure and re-engineering
Reopened as toll road 2005

s: Where Britain took the Cape — under a milkwood tree
w: A centuries-old milkwood at Treaty and Spring Streets, Woodstock, marks where the Batavian Republic surrendered the Cape Colony to Britain on 10 January 1806 after the Battle of Blaauwberg. Before that, it was the site of a slave market and public executions until 1834.
_facts: Old milkwood tree at Treaty and Spring Streets, Woodstock (formerly Papendorp)
British-Batavian articles of capitulation signed January 10 1806 after Battle of Blaauwberg
Britain gained Cape Colony on this date
Also site of slave sales and executions until 1834

s: Urban farm on Cape Town's oldest suburban plot
w: The suburb of Oranjezicht takes its name — 'orange view' — from the view of the Castle's Orange bastion this farm once enjoyed. Established in 1708, it supplied fresh produce to the Castle for nearly two centuries. Cleared for housing in the 1960s; revived as a community farm in 2013.
_facts: Former Oranjezicht farm established 1708
Oranjezicht means 'orange view' (view of Orange bastion of Castle of Good Hope)
Supplied fresh produce to the Castle for approximately 200 years
Main farmhouse demolished 1960s
Community urban farm re-established 2013

s: The beach where Britain seized South Africa
w: On 6-7 January 1806, some 5,399 British troops under General David Baird landed on this stretch of sand. Two days later, at the Battle of Blaauwberg, the Dutch Batavians surrendered. Britain held the Cape from that day until the Union of South Africa was formed in 1910.
_facts: British forces landed January 6-7 1806, approximately 5,399 men under General David Baird
Battle of Blaauwberg fought January 8 1806
Dutch Batavians surrendered Cape Colony
Led to British control until Union of South Africa 1910
Offers famous view of Table Mountain across Table Bay

s: SA's first forestry experiment, declared monument
w: South Africa's first large-scale silviculture experiment was planted here in 1885, to test whether imported timber species could grow in the Cape. The 14-hectare arboretum was declared a National Monument in 1985, on its centenary. A Gondwana Garden added in the 1990s plants 100-million-year-old species.
_facts: First large-scale silviculture experimental station in South Africa
Planted 1885
14 hectares main arboretum area
Declared National Monument 1985 on its centenary
Gondwana Garden created 1990s displaying plants from 100 million years ago
